# Good Friday 2026 — Hormuz, Lebanon, Islamabad talks, FT oil panic — Alexander Mercouris (digest)

---

## Perceiver — neutral summary (≤200 words)

Mercouris (2026-04-10, Orthodox **Good Friday**) frames a fragile **U.S.–Iran** (and **GCC**) truce: no reported **U.S.–Iran** exchanges; **Lebanon** (**Israel**–**Hezbollah**) fighting continues, with civilian toll and **Merkava** damage claims—**Israel** said to talk less about occupying south of the **Litani**. **Iran** retains territorial control and, in his reading, tight **Strait of Hormuz** management (traffic largely with **Iranian** permission), deepening a global energy squeeze. He quotes the **Financial Times** on **North Sea** spot stress (**Forties** blend near **$147**/bbl vs **Brent** **June** ~**$97**), **Amos Hochstein** on market psychology if the strait is priced as indefinitely closed, and summarizes an **Iranian**-published **Hormuz** analysis as claiming indefinite leverage, tolls, and resistance to coercion. **Joe Kent** (prior **Duran**) on garrisoning choke points; **Islamabad** talks (**JD Vance**, **Witkoff**, **Kushner**) with **Iranian** reluctance while **Lebanon** fires; **Russia** cited for ceasefire covering **Lebanon**. **GCC** splits (**Qatar**/**Oman** vs **Bahrain**/**UAE**; **Saudi** hedging); first **Iran–Saudi** FM contact since **28 Feb**. Transatlantic stress (**Trump**/**Rutte**, **Greenland**, **European** Gulf deployment expectations per **Der Spiegel**); **Dmitriev** visit (**Peskov**: not **Ukraine** talks); **Putin** Easter truce in **Ukraine**; **UK** shadow-fleet seizure plan reportedly dropped. Long **Donbas** / **Sumy** / **Zaporozhye** scenario tail—analyst war commentary, not verified order of battle.

## Full transcript

*Light cleanup: names (**Hochstein**, **Araghchi**, **Ghalibaf**, **Rozivka**, **Orikhiv**), institution spellings; substantive wording unchanged.*

---

Good day. Today is Friday, 10th April 2026, which in the Orthodox Christian world — to which I belong — is Good Friday. A very important day for Orthodox Christians, just as Western Good Friday is for Western Christians.

Obviously today is a day which I am going to devote mostly to the events, the thoughts, and the actions that are appropriate for such a day in advance of the great Easter holiday, which happens on Sunday.

I should say in advance that I will not be making a program on Easter Day unless something very great and very dramatic happens. Easter Day is absolutely a day which I am going to give to my family. So, in anticipation, just to confirm: I don't expect to be making a program on Sunday. Obviously I am making a program today, but it is being done within the constraints of today's festival.

Tomorrow, Easter Monday, I expect to be making a program very much as normal.

Anyway, with all that said, let me now proceed to the topics that I am going to discuss in today's program.

Briefly, the fragile truce between Iran and the United States — and by the way, between Iran and the Persian Gulf countries — is just about holding.

I am not hearing any reports of military action by the Americans and the Iranians against each other. I have heard no reports of missile strikes or other attacks on Iran either by the US or the Israeli air forces, or indeed the air force of the UAE, which apparently was also briefly involved in some aerial operations against Iran. There were certainly reports to that effect.

For their part, the Iranians have not been conducting missile and drone strikes against any military or other facilities controlled by the United States across the Middle East. And so far they have not conducted missile strikes against Israel. So in that respect, the ceasefire which was announced on Tuesday is holding.

However, fighting in Lebanon between Israel and Hezbollah continues. Israel continues to bomb Lebanon severely. I have heard reports that some of this bombing is now extending beyond the Shia areas where Hezbollah draws its support. I am not sure why Israel wishes to conduct bombing in that way.

There are some reports — in fact, I believe this to be true — that Israel and the Lebanese government are going to have talks, perhaps tomorrow. The problem with that, of course, is that the party Israel is in conflict with is not the Lebanese government, but the Hezbollah movement in Lebanon. It is not clear to me exactly what the purpose of these talks between Israel and the Lebanese government actually is.

Having said that, Hezbollah does exert very considerable political influence inside Lebanon. It would be an exaggeration to say that it dominates Lebanese politics — Lebanon, as many know, is a deeply fragmented country — but certainly Hezbollah does have some influence over the Lebanese government. There are probably members of that government who are close to Hezbollah, and it might be the case that this negotiation between Israel and the Lebanese government is a kind of way for the Israelis to negotiate indirectly with Hezbollah without appearing to do so.

It would probably be politically extremely difficult for Israel to negotiate directly with Hezbollah, given the kind of statements that Israeli officials have for many years made about Hezbollah, referring to it essentially as a terrorist movement.

So anyway, we will see what happens. But for the moment at least, the fighting in Lebanon continues. There are reports of hundreds of civilians having been killed in the bombing. The bombing remains very intense. There continues to be fighting south of the Litani River in southern Lebanon. There are reports that the Israelis are finding the fighting extremely difficult and that Hezbollah is putting up a determined resistance. There have been many reports of damage and destruction of Israeli Merkava tanks.

I have noticed that the Israelis seem to be talking rather less than they did about capturing and occupying all of Lebanon south of the Litani River. I am not sure whether that plan has been formally abandoned, but it does not look as if it is achievable. The Israelis, for the moment at least, seem to be talking about it a lot less.

So there we are. That is the military situation, if you will, in the Middle East.

But the most important single fact that emerged out of this conflict — the one that Israel and the United States decided to initiate on the 28th of February 2026 in the midst of what looked to be successful negotiations — remains the same. The government and political system in Iran remains intact. The Iranian government and armed forces remain intact. The Iranian government and the Iranian armed forces retain complete control of all of Iranian territory.

And centrally, crucially, critically, most importantly: Iran continues to exercise tight control of the Strait of Hormuz.

Apparently there are still no ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz in any quantity. Those that do pass do so with the permission of the Iranian authorities. And the global energy crisis that I have discussed in many programs — as a result — continues to deepen.

Yesterday there was an article in the Financial Times, an article which I should say bears a sense of panic about it: "North Sea oil prices hit record high as Iran keeps hold over Strait of Hormuz. Washington-Tehran ceasefire agreement fails to stem global energy crunch."

It goes on to read: a rush among European and Asian refineries to secure oil has pushed North Sea prices to a record high as Iran's stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz triggers fresh angst in the market. The 40's blend, a marker for oil for immediate delivery, hit almost $147 a barrel on Thursday — above the highs reached on the eve of the 2008 financial crisis — as traders scrap for oil cargos to replace huge volumes now trapped in the Gulf. The physical barrels for the North Sea were trading far above the roughly $97 a barrel price of Brent, the international benchmark for delivery in June. Another sign of fear of shortages in the oil market.

It then notes that there are mounting signs of stress and that Asia is especially vulnerable to disruption because 80% of the oil and petroleum products it needs transits the Strait of Hormuz.

There is a comment from Amos Hochstein, who the Financial Times says was an energy advisor to former US President Joe Biden. He is quoted as saying: "If this continues for a few more days, we can see the market deciding that the straits are closed indefinitely, which could lead not only to an increase in price, but to a crisis in Asia. This is not just about high prices. This is about an actual physical shortage which is playing out."

Now Amos Hochstein is of course a former advisor of Joe Biden's. He is obviously not going to be a friend of Donald Trump's. He is not going to say things that appear to help Donald Trump. But for the record, what he is recorded as saying here by the Financial Times is that if there is no diplomatic or political movement to get the Strait of Hormuz reopened for traffic on the same basis that existed before 28th February 2026, when the conflict began, then the market is going to give up all hope that that is going to change — unless it happens over the next few days. They will decide that the Iranians are going to maintain control of the Strait of Hormuz indefinitely, and that in itself is going to have a massive psychological effect on the markets. They will begin to price oil futures differently, and we could find ourselves facing a physical shortage.

By coincidence, the Iranians themselves have now published what they say is an analysis of the situation in the Strait of Hormuz. Let me stress this is an entirely Iranian-produced document. But what it says is this: "Why no power can undermine Iran's eternal dominance over the Strait of Hormuz."

It continues: "The Strait of Hormuz, a strategic waterway nestled between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is not merely a geographical passageway or a shipping lane on the world map to the Islamic Republic of Iran. It is a strategically vital waterway that forms the pulse of the global energy economy and simultaneously a potent asset for the Islamic Republic to fundamentally reshape the balance of power in the Persian Gulf and around the world."

So the Iranians can see — they now know — that control of the Strait of Hormuz, the kind of control that they are exercising, enables them to reshape fundamentally the balance of power in the Persian Gulf and around the world.

The analysis goes on to say that Iran seeks not merely to protect or monitor the Strait of Hormuz, but to exercise absolute, intelligent and legitimate control that in the short term applies economic pressure on any adversary to force it into retreat, negotiation or acceptance of Iranian terms, and in the long term to convert this control into permanent and inexhaustible strategic advantage.

This unchallenged authority on the strategic choke point — which carries around a quarter of global seaborne oil trade — includes regulating maritime traffic, collecting passage tolls, influencing global supply chains, and reconfiguring power dynamics in the region in alignment with the Axis of Resistance. In other words, in alignment with the interests and desires of Iran and its regional allies.

It continues that, backed by immutable geographical realities, international legal frameworks, precise economic data, and Iran's asymmetric military capabilities, no military threats nor diplomatic pressure can affect this fundamental and unalterable reality.

Iran is uniquely positioned to exert absolute control over the northern and most critical part of the strait, with its coastline stretching more than 1,600 kilometres along the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman. This extensive coastline includes not only mainland shores but also numerous strategic islands that serve as natural strong points.

Unlike the Suez Canal or the Panama Canal, the Strait of Hormuz is the only natural mandatory route for crude oil, LNG, and chemical products exiting the Persian Gulf en route to the Indian Ocean and global markets. No viable alternative to bypass Iran's control exists.

So that is what the Iranians are now saying. They are saying that they have this chokehold over the global economy. They are going to exert it indefinitely. They are going to levy tolls. They are going to control shipping. They understand the enormous power that control of the Strait of Hormuz gives them. They don't believe that there is any military force on earth that can deprive them of it, and they intend to retain control of it indefinitely.

And that apparently is one of the conditions they are going to make in the negotiations.

So there you have it. The Iranians have discovered this extraordinary source of power, this extraordinary source of wealth. This has been one of the outcomes — in fact, in some respects the single most important outcome — of the 40-day war that was fought after the 28th of February. And they are never going to surrender it again. Why would they?

That is the biggest, most important, salient fact that we are looking at now.

President Trump has made more threatening words about the unacceptability of the Iranians retaining control of the Strait of Hormuz in the way that they say they are going to do. The Iranians, judging from this article, appear confident that they can defy Donald Trump.

In other statements that the Iranians have been making over the last 36 hours, they say that they have fought the United States to a point of strategic exhaustion, that the Americans are indeed out of weapons, that the United States dare not risk a military campaign to force open the Strait of Hormuz.

The Iranians may be making big mistakes in making those assumptions, but they are the assumptions they are making now. And to say it straightforwardly, I don't think that there is any pressure anybody can bring to bear now that is going to make them change their view. I don't think there is any diplomatic or economic pressure, because the economic pressure that Iran is able to exert is so much greater now than any pressure which can be brought to bear against it.

The only way that this situation can change is if the United States commits overwhelming force and is prepared to wage a long, indefinite campaign to force open the Strait of Hormuz.

Yesterday on The Duran we were privileged to do a program with Joe Kent, the former director of counterterrorism in President Trump's administration. Joe Kent made the point that even if the United States were able to occupy all of the islands and all of the immediate territory so as to succeed in opening the Strait of Hormuz, it would at that point find itself in a situation where it would have to garrison these control points indefinitely, lest the Iranians choose to regain control. And that would mean permanent boots on the ground on this very territory, facing continuous threat of Iranian retaliation.

Joe Kent at least seemed doubtful that this would be acceptable politically to the American people in the United States.

So there it is. This is where we are.

The Iranians say that for the moment at least they will continue to exercise extremely stringent controls over exit and entry through the Strait of Hormuz until and unless the fighting in Lebanon ends. And they are also saying that they reserve the right to restart the war, to conduct missile and drone strikes if the fighting in Lebanon continues — which, were it to happen, would at least in the short term compound the atmosphere of crisis even further.

I am going to say it: it is increasingly looking to me as if that decision to start this conflict on the 28th of February was the most catastrophic decision that has been made by any Western government since the end of the Second World War.

It has delivered a blow to the collective West and to its system of international friendships and alliances such as we have never seen. It has quite plausibly weakened in the long term the position of the dollar as the global reserve currency. There is even now an article in the Financial Times saying as much. And it has massively strengthened an adversary of the United States — the Islamic Republic of Iran — which is now increasingly looking as if it is going to assume a dominant position in the Middle East.

Lastly, it has created tensions between the United States and many of its allies, between the United States and the Persian Gulf states, which feel that the American security guarantee that they have relied upon for so many decades has failed them.

There are apparently now tensions between some of the Persian Gulf states. Qatar and Oman are apparently now arguing that it is now imperative to improve relations with Iran and to work towards some kind of reconciliation with Iran. Bahrain and the UAE take a harder line against Iran. They seem to be still urging the Americans to take more military action against Iran in some form. The Saudis — MBS — have been unable to make a decision between the two. They are prevaricating.

But perhaps the first sign that the Saudis are starting to understand the balance of realities in the Middle East is that yesterday, for the first time since the conflict started on the 28th of February 2026, the Iranian and Saudi foreign ministers had a discussion with each other. And of course it is a discussion in which Iran now is in a position of overwhelming advantage.

So that is one alliance which is starting to fracture.

Another alliance which is now experiencing, I suspect, growing stress is the one between the United States and Israel. The Israelis appear to have assumed that if they could involve the United States in a war against Iran, then it would be a repeat of what happened in Iraq in 2003. It would result in the collapse of the Iranian government. A number of people — Joe Kent for example, in the program we did yesterday — said that the Israelis are less concerned by what sort of government is established in Iran. If there is a collapse there, if there is chaos as there was in Iraq, well, they can live with that.

But what they have got instead is a stronger, emboldened, more powerful Iran. American power did not achieve the outcome that Israel expected.

On the American side — and indeed on the Western side in general — one senses growing exasperation with Israel's apparent obsession with waging a conflict with Iran that has brought the entire collective West to the point of disaster which we now see.

Remarkably, in the Daily Telegraph — perhaps the most pro-Israeli newspaper in Britain — there is now an opinion article published by one of the Daily Telegraph's permanent stable of writers, David Blair, in which he speaks about the need for the United States to rein in the Israelis. When articles like that begin to appear even in places like the Daily Telegraph, then you can sense that the ground is shifting.

Last but not least, there is a major crisis — or at least an apparent crisis — developing between the United States and its European allies. Apparently over the course of the meeting between Donald Trump and Mark Rutte, the NATO Secretary General, that I spoke about in my program yesterday, Donald Trump again brought up the subject of Greenland, just as he did in a recent post that he published on Truth Social.

And Donald Trump has again apparently demanded that the Europeans send military forces to the Persian Gulf to assist the Americans to force open the Strait of Hormuz. There is a long piece about this in the German media magazine Der Spiegel.

What exactly Donald Trump expects the Europeans to achieve is very difficult for me to say.

For the record, there are reports of continued US military deployments to the Middle East. There is supposed to be more American infantry arriving. Though it still seems to be a case of light infantry — we haven't yet seen evidence of the major buildup of heavy infantry and tanks and armoured vehicles and those sort of things that would be needed to conduct some kind of long-term ground operation against Iran.

My sense is that for the moment at least, Donald Trump really doesn't want to go there. Though, of course, with such an unpredictable president, one cannot be certain that that might not change.

But anyway, the Americans are increasingly angry with their European allies, apparently shocked by the revelation of their allies' profound military weakness — which has been obvious to all of us who have been monitoring the military realities in Europe for a long time. The Americans, perhaps until recently, still shaped by memories of the very large military force that the British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher sent to the Middle East in 1990 to support the operation to drive the Iraqis out of Kuwait, and the rather smaller but still significant force that the British Prime Minister Tony Blair sent to the Middle East to support George W. Bush's operation to oust Saddam Hussein in 2003.

The Americans might have assumed that their European allies were in a position to put together forces of that kind of scale. It has come as a shock to them to discover that their European allies no longer have those sort of military capabilities and are never going to regain them — not in any realistic future.

So the Americans are deeply dismayed by the weakness, the feebleness of their allies, and perhaps starting to wonder whether their allies in Europe are a strategic liability rather than an asset.

And the allies themselves — the European allies themselves — are anxiously worrying amongst themselves about whether it really is the end of the road between them and the United States, when the Americans are finally on the brink of losing patience and might finally make the decision to leave. Something which, to be clear, most European leaders would look upon with horror.

And of course, if Donald Trump does come back after this debacle in the Middle East and does reopen the question of Greenland, all of these questions, all of these fears, all of these doubts are going to be increased still further.

And just to cap it off, there is also for Americans the situation in East Asia to worry about and think about. The fact that the Japanese, as I have discussed in several programs, are desperately knocking on the Russians' door trying to get the Russians to agree to provide them with oil and gas. The fact that the leader of the Kuomintang opposition in Taiwan is now going to Beijing to try to mend fences with the Chinese leadership and Xi Jinping. The fact that other allies of the United States like the Philippines apparently are asking the Chinese for help with refined oil products which they can no longer receive from the Persian Gulf.

It is difficult to imagine a set of decisions made by Western leaders that have had more disastrous consequences than this.

The previous worst decision was the set of decisions that Joe Biden and the European leadership made in February 2022 when they first provoked the Russians into starting the special military operation in Ukraine and then launched a catastrophic economic war against Russia which has triggered de-industrialisation across Europe and which has ruptured the relationship between Europe and Russia perhaps indefinitely — or I suspect for most of my lifetime.

But this decision to begin — without proper assessment or calculation — the conflict against Iran on the 28th of February, if possible, goes further and exceeds that folly.

So there we are.

Now, today the 10th of April was supposed to be the day when negotiations — formal negotiations — between the United States and Iran in Islamabad were supposed to begin. And I gather that the US Vice President JD Vance is in Islamabad waiting to meet with the Iranians. And Witkoff and Kushner are also still there.

The Iranians apparently have made known their deep unhappiness about the presence of those two individuals on the American negotiating team. The Iranians have spoken about who their leading negotiators would be, and they are Araghchi, the foreign minister, and Ghalibaf, the speaker of the Iranian parliament — the man who Donald Trump a short time ago seemed to think was the more reasonable, more moderate leader, though in fact and in truth I have never seen a scintilla of evidence to support that assertion.

But as of the time of making this program, I am not even sure whether the Iranian representatives are even in Islamabad and whether they do actually intend to meet the Americans today.

I say that because there continue to be reports in the Iranian media that the Iranians are sending out messages that they don't see any point in conducting negotiations with the Americans whilst the fighting in Lebanon remains underway. If there is going to be a negotiation, that has to be premised on the ceasefire that was agreed on Tuesday being in effect. As far as the Iranians are concerned, the ceasefire extends to Lebanon. They have the support of the Pakistanis in this. By the way, they also have the support of the Russians. The Russian Foreign Ministry said today that as far as Russia is concerned, the ceasefire extends and covers Lebanon also.

So the Iranians are saying that there has to be a full implementation of the ceasefire — which means an end to the fighting in Lebanon — in order to get the talks started. And until that happens they see no point in having those talks.

President Trump may threaten and thunder and warn as much as he likes, but that remains the Iranian position.

Well, I am going to make a guess here. I still think that despite these additional American troop deployments, the United States is not in a realistic position at the present time to restart the fighting in the Persian Gulf. It does not want the ceasefire to collapse. So it probably will, over the next couple of hours and/or days, start to apply more pressure on its Israeli ally to get the Israelis to stop operations in Lebanon. They will obviously run into fierce resistance from their Israeli allies, but I expect eventually the authority of the United States to prevail.

But to repeat again: even once that happens, as things stand, given the Iranian stance, given the demands that the Iranians are making, given the Iranian insistence on maintaining indefinite permanent control of the Strait of Hormuz, the Americans are coming to the negotiating table in Islamabad from a position of weakness.

One must now at least address seriously the possibility — probability even — that at the end of this conflict we will have a situation where the Iranians do indeed retain control of the Strait of Hormuz and succeed in bending the Persian Gulf states to their will. And that will be a geopolitical revolution to say the least. And it will also be a fundamental restructuring of the balance of power in the global economy.

Well, there we are. That is how things look to me today. We still get, as I said, all these reports about an American military buildup. Maybe that will change the whole situation, but for the moment at least, I think it is unlikely.

Now, the other powers — the Russians — are watching all of these events in the Middle East very carefully. Their envoy Kirill Dmitriev, the head of the Russian Direct Investment Fund, he is again traveling to Washington. However, Peskov, Putin's spokesman, has said very clearly that Dmitriev is not going to the United States in order to negotiate on the subject of Ukraine. There are no negotiations between the United States and Russia, and between Russia and Ukraine, taking place at the present time. And Dmitriev is not going to the United States with that purpose.

Dmitriev is going to the United States to discuss bilateral economic matters. And here again, the Russians are now in a position of advantage because, as a result of the oil shortage, the energy shortage, the United States has already had to lift — temporarily, as it says — the sanctions on Russian oil, on the global trade in Russian oil. The word is that President Trump is going to do this again.

Dmitriev almost certainly is going to Washington and he is going to say that in this situation the sanctions need to be lifted completely — that it makes no sense, given the global energy situation and given that already the inflation rate in the United States has risen to 3.3% on an annualized basis (I know there are many people who think that is an underestimate).

Dmitriev is definitely going to go to the United States and is going to say that it makes no sense to keep those energy sanctions — those sanctions on trading in Russian oil — in place at all. He may very well insist that the sanctions on Rosneft and Lukoil — the two big Russian oil companies that the United States imposed in November — be lifted.

And the Russians might go further still and they might say that yes, there is this global energy shortage, but perhaps the way to resolve it in the long term is to work with us. We have huge untapped oil reserves, especially in the Arctic. Once upon a time, not so long ago, your company Exxon was going to help us develop those oil reserves. Think now about whether you might want to come back. Only this time, of course, we will want definite, long-term, indefinite guarantees that in the future, whatever happens in the world of geopolitics, there will be no step taken by the United States in terms of sanctions or tariffs that will interfere with investments by companies like Exxon in the Russian oil industry.

Now, saying all of that, I should say that I have received many reports and a lot of information that the Kremlin has given warnings to Russian companies — including oil companies — to have as little to do with American and European companies as possible. That the Russian intelligence agencies have assessed that many of these American and European companies were covertly undertaking intelligence gathering in Russia on behalf of their governments.

And I don't believe that the Russians seriously expect the Americans to lift any sanctions. And I don't believe that the Russians actually do really want the Americans back in Russia, investing in the Russian oil industry. But I strongly suspect that Dmitriev is nonetheless going to say these things. Not because he expects the Americans to agree, and not because the Russians want the Americans to agree, but because the Russians again want to show to the Americans how strong they are, how resource-rich they are, how it is absolutely not in America's interests to undertake these constant economic collisions with them.

And the Russians are probably also going to tell the Americans something else. I have been reading in the Russian media that another oil tanker is now being filled up in Russia, intending to steam to Cuba to deliver another big cargo of Russian oil. And the Russians are also saying that a big Cuban delegation is now expected to visit Russia to attend the forthcoming St. Petersburg International Economic Forum.

So the Russians not only are breaching the American oil embargo on Cuba, but they are now working hard to do exactly that which I have talked about in many previous programs: to build up economic links with Cuba, ultimately with a view to integrating Cuba into the BRICS economic system, which they believe is both to Cuba's advantage and of course to their own.

So that is underway too.

Now Peskov has been talking about other things. He confirmed what many expected: that President Putin would announce a truce in the fighting in Ukraine over the Easter holiday. This is a relatively short truce, by the way, and Zelensky, who is anxious for a truce, has said that he agrees to it.

Putin always comes under significant pressure from the Orthodox Church to agree to these truces ahead of religious holidays — Orthodox religious holidays. The military is never enthusiastic about them, to put it mildly. But Putin, who is of course himself a practicing Orthodox Christian, in the end always agrees.

This truce, as I said, is particularly short this year, and I doubt that it is going to have any significance on the Ukrainian battle lines.

Peskov however took the opportunity, when he confirmed that this Easter truce was indeed in place, to reiterate that Russia is not interested in any temporary ceasefires, as he put it. In other words, prolonged ceasefires lasting two weeks, three weeks, a month, three months or whatever — the Russians still see that as nothing more or less than an attempt by the Ukrainians to re-equip and rearm and an opportunity for Macron and Starmer to insert British and French troops into western Ukraine. So the Russians are not going to agree to that.

Peskov again said that the Russians will not agree to any sort of cessation of hostilities unless and until there is a full and complete final agreement of the conflict between Ukraine and Russia, which must be based on Russian proposals, on Russian terms. And of course, as Zelensky himself repeatedly says, that is something that Ukraine is not ever going to agree to.

Now, the Russians are not only going to insist on that, but just as they are feeling in an increasingly strong position relative to the Americans, they are feeling in an even stronger position relative to the Europeans.

In my program yesterday I spoke about how the British had been upset — humiliated even — by the passage of tankers laden with Russian oil steaming through British waters or waters close to Britain, I should more accurately say, guarded by the Russian Navy — the Admiral Grigorovich frigate.

And well, a report has appeared in the British media that the British government has quietly decided that it is going to drop its plan to try to seize ships of the so-called shadow fleet trading in Russian oil on the high seas. Apparently the Attorney General, Lord Hermer, has advised that yes, there are some circumstances where this would be legally acceptable, but that this isn't something that should usually be done and he feels that this is something that on balance the British should not do.

In fact and in reality, the British are in no position to defy by themselves the Russian navy, the Russian military, on the high seas. Throughout the conflict in Ukraine, the British have talked very big. They have talked about the assistance they provided Ukraine. They have talked about the various measures they are supposedly taking to support Ukraine. They have obviously provided a significant amount of military equipment. They ridiculed at every opportunity the performance and capability of the Russian armed forces.

But in reality, when confronted by the actual military force that the Russians can bring to bear, the British know that they are comprehensively — even overwhelmingly — outmatched. This has been a depressing realization for many people in London. I think shock is perhaps too strong a word because I suspect that most people in London in high authority deep down have always known it. But certainly it is a depressing thing finally to have to face.

But there it is. And of course, if the Americans really are thinking of reducing their footprint in Europe, well, that is going to leave Britain and the European states in an even weaker position in any future confrontations — or indeed in the existing confrontation — with the Russians.

So anyway, that is the British.

But in the meantime, the war in Ukraine continues. I discussed in my program yesterday how the Russians are likely to intensify their offensive after the Easter holiday. There has been apparently quite a significant amount of movement now in some places. There are reports that the Russians have now captured a significant number of positions around the city of Sumy in the north.

The operations around Sumy in northeastern Ukraine are intriguing and they do beg a number of questions. Stanislav Krapivnik has previously pointed out that if the Russians do capture and occupy Sumy, that cuts the much bigger and far more important city of Kharkov from its main route of supply from central Ukraine. But the capture of Sumy also importantly opens the road to Kiev. There is apparently very little in the way of barriers between Sumy and Kiev, and in theory at least the Russian army could trundle along the main motorway. Obviously there would be Ukrainian resistance along the way, but they could trundle along the main motorway and eventually they would get to Kiev. And there are very few natural fortified positions that the Ukrainians could rely upon to slow their advance. This is the advance that the Russians mostly carried out in February and March 2022. Some Russian forces moved towards Kiev from the north, from Belarus. But perhaps the larger force reached Kiev advancing towards Kiev through Sumy and along the motorway, taking up positions close to Kiev on the east bank of the Dnieper.

Well, I suspect that is a battle to come, but the Russians are now significantly extending their control in northeastern Ukraine, in the Sumy region, in ways that seem to me only explicable if an operation to capture the city of Sumy is indeed being prepared.

So that is one thing. But obviously the main focus continues to be Donbass and Zaporozhye. These remain the major battlefields, as I discussed in my program yesterday.

The Russians have now reached the strategically important town of Rozivka. There is likely to be a big battle to capture this town over the next couple of days. And the Russians seem to be breaking through in the Zaporozhye region. Around there, they seem to be breaking through the defences of the town of Orikhiv. And of course once Orikhiv is captured, the way is open to advance further towards the Dnieper and to lay siege to Zaporozhye as well.

Now if all of these things happen this year — if the Russians capture Sumy, if the Russians occupy the remainder of Donbass, if they capture these two big remaining towns, Sloviansk and Kramatorsk, and they are positioning themselves to encircle them, if they capture Orikhiv and reach the Dnieper — well, Ukraine at that point is in a strategic crisis. It would actually be an existential moment.

A Ukraine without Donbass is viable. A Ukraine without Kharkov is viable. A Ukraine where the Russian army is pitching tents in central Ukraine on the Dnieper and is within striking distance in the north of Kiev is a Ukraine which — especially given the probable lack by that time of external support — whose very future is at that point in serious doubt.

We are moving closer to that point, and we are moving closer to that point very fast. We are now in April. As I said, there is still the Easter holiday. Probably we won't see decisive events playing out until late summer, early autumn, as I discussed in my program yesterday.

But there is now a very plausible scenario — some would say a probable scenario — whereby we could have a global economic crisis by late autumn, or at least a very severe recession in the global economy, in the United States and in the collective West; where we could have the Iranians still in control of Hormuz, still in a dominant position in the Middle East; and we could have the Russians on the Dnieper.

This would be the biggest crisis that the collective West has experienced since the end of the Second World War. It would be a crisis on multiple fronts. I don't know. I cannot imagine what would happen then. Some might expect it to be a consolidating moment — a moment where the Europeans and the Americans, faced with a crisis of that type, come together and consolidate and think of some kind of plan.

But to be frank, both the Europeans and the Americans at the moment seem far too fragmented, far too divided. Their leadership far too confused and inadequate to face a moment like that.

Well, I don't want to overstate things, especially so far in advance of events. But as we approach the Orthodox Easter, it does increasingly seem to me as if 2026 is indeed going to be the single most important year we have seen since the end of the Second World War — at the very least — and one which will be seen as representing a pivot point in history.

Well, that is me for today. Let me remind you again: you can find all our programs on our various platforms — Locals, Rumble and X. You can support our work via Patreon and SubscribeStar and by going to our shop links under this video. Don't forget to check out our Substack site.

Last but not least, if you've liked this video, please remember to tick the like button and to check your subscription to this channel.

That's me for today. More from me soon. Have a very good day.
